title: Needle-felted moon hare
subtitle: A seated hare in pale grey merino with long ears: a traditional British motif
excerpt: A seated hare is a classic needle-felting subject. This version stands about 8 cm tall in pale grey merino with darker grey ear linings and a white belly. The project covers full-body 3D shaping, ear construction on a wire frame, and surface-detail work for facial features. Finished piece is self-supporting on a flat base.
sourceNotes: Needle-felted hare figures are an established subject in British craft instruction. Technique is observation-led.

content.text: A seated hare is built from three separate pieces: a pear-shaped body (the combined torso and haunches), a head, and two long ears on wire armatures. The body is made first and provides a stable base for the other elements.
work refines the form in the final stage.

attrs.body: Always stab straight in and out. Keep a foam pad under the work; never stab toward the hand holding the piece.
attrs.title: Needle safety

items.name: Merino roving, mid grey (ear linings and shadows)
Merino roving, white (belly and eye ring)

content.text: Take approximately 20 g of pale grey roving. Form a pear shape with the wider end at the bottom (the haunches) and the narrower end at the top (the shoulders). Needle all over with the size-36 needle, rotating the piece frequently. Work toward a compact form about 6 cm tall and 4 cm across at the widest point.

Form a ball of approximately 5 g of pale grey roving. Needle into an oval about 2.5 cm long and 2 cm wide. The nose end should be very slightly pointed; the back of the skull should be rounded. Needle the head until firm.

Cut two 10-cm wire lengths. Bend each into a long narrow oval with a 0.5-cm base loop at the bottom. Lay a thin wisp of pale grey roving on the foam, place the wire frame on top, and cover with another thin wisp. Needle through both layers and the wire frame until the wool holds to the wire. Add a central strip of mid grey along the inner face of each ear. Needle flat with the fine needle.
Step 4: Assemble head, ears, and body

Needle the head onto the top of the body, working around the join from all sides. Insert the wire base loops of the ears into the top of the head and needle the wool at the base to secure them. The ears should stand upright and bend naturally.

Apply a patch of white roving to the front of the body as the belly. Needle flat with the size-40 needle. Add small black wool dots for the eyes and a tiny black triangle for the nose. Blend the edges of the white belly into the grey body with the fine needle until the boundary is soft.

attrs.intro: Two common problems when making 3D animal figures.

items.fix: Work the join from all angles: front, back, both sides, and slightly from above. Equal pressure from all directions keeps the head centred.
items.cause: The join between head and body was needled only from the front, pulling the head forward.
items.symptom: Head sits at an unnatural angle after needling

items.fix: Use 0.5 mm wire. Thinner wire holds the felt shape but is still bendable by hand for positioning.
items.cause: Wire armature is too thick (above 0.8 mm).
items.symptom: Ears too stiff to arrange naturally

attrs.heading: Common problems
